class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Noreen Boyle's pipes predate that moment.</p><p>"My pipes date back to 1943," she said. "I've had them since 1980."</p><p>Though refurbished, the bags are synthetic now.</p><p>"Some people still play sheepskin bags," she said. "When I started, you'd have to season them with honey, to keep it supple. A messy thing. When it came to the Gore-Tex bags, we thought we had died and gone to heaven."</p><p>Between the loud skirling and the sticky honey, I imagined a child taking up the bagpipes might not be the most welcome development in a crowded household. I thought wrong.</p><p>"I'm a second-generation American," said Noreen Boyle, who grew up in St. Tarcissus Parish on the Northwest Side. "My grandmother was from Limerick. She used to go down to the Old St. Patrick's corned beef dinner every year. I'm from a family of six kids, and we'd each get a turn to go down there. My turn, the Shannon Rovers were there. And I remember them starting out, and I guess I was fascinated. I don't know how my dad knew Tommie Ryan, but he was friendly with him. He asked if he would teach me the bagpipes."</p><p>Ryan was a heroic figure who fought for the Irish Republican Army and was deported to the United States in 1923, ending up in Chicago, where he started the Rovers in 1926. They quickly became part of the social and political life of the city, playing for Franklin D. Roosevelt in 1939 and most presidents since, changing their tartans from Gordon to Kennedy Modern after performing at the funeral of John F. Kennedy.</p><p>The Rovers play about 400 gigs a year — 66 were scheduled for Saturday. July 9 there is a centennial celebration at Soldier Field. The full contingent plays only at parades and the annual gala; most feature only a piper or two, maybe a drummer.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7e0000" name="image-7e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e0de261/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee9f302/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23ced05/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Shannon Rovers Pipe Band opens the 26th annual Chicago Polar Plunge at North Avenue Beach, Sunday, March 1, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23ced05/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/002a0b9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23ced05/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8640x4849+0+457/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2Ff1%2F338bbffd49c596e1891248906745%2Fshannon-rovers-polar-plunge-260302-003.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Shannon Rovers Pipe Band play hundreds of events every year — all sorts of events, though the full contingent shows up only for the biggest ones. Earlier this month, some of the Rovers kicked off the the 26th annual Chicago Polar Plunge at North Avenue Beach.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The benefits of membership are many. The music, the kinship. Even the pleasure of getting ready.</p><p>"You do feel like you're armored up," McAndrew said.</p><p>And the places they find themselves — in parades, before presidents, at the House of Blues with Rod Stewart. They participate in events from raucous dinners to solemn funerals.</p><p>"The Vietnam Veterans parade" in 1986, one of the most emotional things I've ever done," " Noreen Boyle said.</p><p>It's all volunteer. Fees go to charity and some group expenses, though musicians have to pay for their bagpipes' four reeds and upkeep on those horsehair sporrans.</p><p>"All incredibly expensive," Noreen Boyd said.</p><p>Which makes the Shannon Rovers' services surprisingly reasonable. If you want a bagpiper to show up at your place of employment to pipe you out to "The Parting Glass" after you tell your boss to take this job and shove it, it'll set you back about $250, depending on the location.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/14/a-rite-of-passage-in-emerald-green" >Chicago’s ‘rite of passage': Crowds flock to watch river turn emerald green</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Cubs righty Jameson Taillon's struggles continue with shellacking by Dodgers
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Cubs right-hander Jameson Taillon's awful spring stayed that way Sunday, with a 10-run shelling from the Dodgers fraught with walks (four), homers (Mookie Betts and Jack Suwinski) and an array of Dodgers hits (eight) over 3 ⅔ innings at Sloan Park.</p><p>Taillon is tinkering with minor mechanical tweaks, but with the season starting in less than two weeks, the time to use that as an excuse is running out, and he knows it.</p><p>“It’s also on me when you’re in a game to go out and compete,” said Taillon, who has been peppered for 23 hits including eight homers in 9 ⅔ innings of work for a 22.18 ERA in Arizona. “Right now I’m not doing that, I’m kind of all over the place. Again, got to be better.”</p><p>It was rough from the get-go, as Taillon walked two and gave up an RBI single to Santiago Espinal and a three-run homer to Suwinski after striking out the game’s first batter, Hyeseong Kim. The Dodgers scored in all four of Taillon’s innings.</p><p>Both homers were off his cutter.</p><p>“Not a ton of deception there,” said Taillon, who characterized the outing as “a disappointing setback.”.</p><p>All signs point to Taillon in the fifth slot of the starting rotation behind Opening Day lefty Matthew Boyd, right-hander Cade Horton, lefty Shota Imanaga and righty Edward Cabrera.</p><h3>Palencia shines</h3><p>Closer Daniel Palencia pitched a perfect ninth with two strikeouts to seal Venezuela’s semifinal win against Japan Saturday night in the WBC. The 26-year-old made six postseason appearances in 2025, and succeeding in the super-charged WBC atmosphere won’t hurt him, either.</p><p>“This tournament is for the experience for sure,” Counsell said. “Does it help you in the next experience? I’m not sure. I don’t know if there is a right answer for that. The experience Daniel had [Saturday] night is a great experience. He’s been in some pretty big moments but it’s not the first one.</p><p>“If anything it makes him glad he did this.”</p><p>Counsell said Palencia, who got Shohei Ohtani to pop out to end the game, “made a big leap” in the offseason.</p><p>“No moments happen in the offseason but it’s that time of ‘What do I need to do to get better at’ without that space of performances lining up the next day,” Counsell said. “There was a big jump made in that time.”</p><h3>Counsell returns</h3><p>Counsell and his wife drove to the airport in “white knuckle” snow and road conditions to the airport bound for Arizona after watching their daughter help White Fish Bay High School win the Division 2 state basketball championship Saturday night in Green Bay, Wis.</p><p>Counsell said he and another team dad coached half the players on the team for five or six years.</p><p>“It was a pretty incredible night,” said Counsell, who missed two days of camp. “I was glad I got to do it. It definitely made memories.</p><p>“You’re happy because they achieved something that will stay with them for a long time.”</p><p>Counsell missed two days in Cubs camp.</p><h3>Dodgers 14, Cubs 8</h3><p>Opening day left-hander Matthew Boyd got dinged for six runs, although two were earned, over 3 ⅓ innings in a minor league game. Boyd allowed eight hits and two walks while striking out five, and he picked off a runner. Boyd threw 74 pitches, 48 strikes. No. 5 starter Jameson Taillon was hit even harder in a loss to the Dodgers that dropped the Cubs’ record to 9-13.</p><p>• Nico Hoerner hit his second homer, a two-run shot that just cleared the left-center field fence, against Emmet Sheehan. Minor league corner infielder Devin Ortiz hit a grand slam in the ninth.</p><p>• Shortstop Dansby Swanson made a leaping catch to stab Alex Call’s liner for an inning-ending out in the second.</p><p>• Top shortstop prospect Jefferson Rojas was OK and went through a normal workout, manager Craig Counsell said, after he jumped for a ball and felt something in his side and left Saturday’s game.</p><p>• On deck: Cubs at Guardians, 8:05 p.m. Monday, Goodyear, Cade Horton vs. Gavin Williams.<br></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: My NCAA Tournament pick: Arizona will win it all in a Big 12-heavy Final Four
entries.summary: <p>It has been a while since my <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >NCAA Tournament</a> bracket had the right champion. That was in 2018, when <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/3/11/18403943/ncaa-tournament-predictions-why-villanova-will-be-the-team-to-beat-for-title" target="_blank" >Villanova was a runaway train</a>. Somehow, I whiffed on Loyola to the Final Four that year.</p><p>In 2021, I had the correct final two, Gonzaga and Baylor, only in the wrong order. Same thing in 2024, when I picked Purdue over UConn. My title pick last year, Houston, finished as runner-up to Florida, which I also projected to reach the Final Four. Too much close but no cigar, if you ask me.</p><p>This time, I’m leaning hard into certain trends that, perhaps, should have guided me before now.</p><p>First, seven of the last eight champs, and 14 of the last 18, have been No. 1 seeds. A strong indicator right there.</p><p>Second, seven straight Most Outstanding Players — all on title winners — have been veterans with three or more years of experience, and six of them have been linchpin guards on No. 1 seeds. We’re talking about stars — Florida’s Walter Clayton Jr., Kansas’ Ochai Agbaji, Baylor’s Jared Butler and so on — with invaluable skill sets and presences.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/illinois-penn-illini-brad-underwood-fran-mccaffery-ncaa-tournament-march-madness" target="_blank" >Illinois a No. 3 seed in NCAA Tournament, will face No. 14 Penn on Thursday in South Carolina</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Beyond that, over the last seven Big Dances, the all-tournament teams have been littered with guards — 23 of them, compared with only 12 forwards and centers. It’s a guard's event, and it certainly tends not to be a coronation of superstar freshman bigs who are bound for the top of the NBA draft as one-and-dones.</p><p>No. 1 seeds Duke, Michigan and Florida don’t check the right boxes. It says here that Duke freshman Cameron Boozer will follow Duke one-and-done, No. 1 overall forwards Zion Williamson (2019), Paolo Banchero (2022) and Cooper Flagg (2025) in falling short on top-seeded teams. And that Michigan and Florida will prove too interior-oriented to go all the way.</p><p>But then there’s Arizona, the only team in the field that checks every box. If you don’t know fourth-year point guard Jaden Bradley yet, you’re going to get to know him over the Wildcats’ next six games. He was the Big 12’s player of the year and its tournament MVP. The last guy to pull that off, Agbaji, went on to win it all, and there’s no reason Bradley — the heartbeat of a 32-2 monster — can’t complete the deal, too.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">JADEN BRADLEY. GAME WINNER. CATS TO THE 'SHIP.<a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/Big12MBB?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#Big12MBB</a> | 📺 ESPN <a href="https://t.co/a9WOAJb9ju">pic.twitter.com/a9WOAJb9ju</a></p>— Big 12 Conference (@Big12Conference) <a href="https://twitter.com/Big12Conference/status/2032625695218602076?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 14, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Bradley is surrounded by extreme talent, with guard Brayden Burries a projected NBA lottery pick, forward Koa Peat a jaw-dropping athlete and 7-2 Motiejus Krivas coming into his own. When the Wildcats need someone to will them over the finish line, that’s what Bradley is for.</p><p>Arizona — admittedly, often a tournament disappointment through the years — is the pick to get it all right and cut down the nets for the first time since 1997.</p><p>You can thank me later.</p><p><b>EAST</b></p><p><b>First-round winners: </b>Duke, Ohio State, St. John’s, Kansas, Louisville, Michigan State, UCF, UConn.</p><p><b>Second round: </b>Duke, St. John’s, Michigan State, UConn.</p><p><b>Sweet 16: </b>Duke, Michigan State.</p><p><b>Elite Eight: </b>Michigan State.</p><p><b>SOUTH</b></p><p><b>First-round winners: </b>Florida (over Lehigh), Iowa, Vanderbilt, Nebraska, VCU, Illinois, Saint Mary’s, Houston.</p><p><b>Second round: </b>Florida, Vanderbilt, Illinois, Houston.</p><p><b>Sweet 16: </b>Florida, Houston.</p><p><b>Elite Eight: </b>Houston.</p><p><b>WEST</b></p><p><b>First-round winners: </b>Arizona, Utah State, Wisconsin, Arkansas, BYU (over NC State), Gonzaga, Miami (FL), Purdue.</p><p><b>Second round: </b>Arizona, Wisconsin, BYU, Purdue.</p><p><b>Sweet 16: </b>Arizona, Purdue.</p><p><b>Elite Eight: </b>Arizona.</p><p><b>MIDWEST</b></p><p><b>First-round winners: </b>Michigan (over UMBC), Saint Louis, Akron, Alabama, Miami (Ohio, over SMU and Tennessee), Virginia, Santa Clara, Iowa State.</p><p><b>Second round: </b>Michigan, Alabama, Virginia, Iowa State.</p><p><b>Sweet 16: </b>Michigan, Iowa State.</p><p><b>Elite Eight: </b>Iowa State.</p><p><b>FINAL FOUR</b></p><p>Houston over Michigan State, Arizona over Iowa State.</p><p><b>CHAMPIONSHIP</b></p><p>Arizona over Houston.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: White Sox' Curtis Mead encouraged after 'eventful' spring with roster cuts looming
entries.summary: <p>SURPRISE, Ariz. — It’s been a busy spring for White Sox third baseman Curtis Mead.</p><p>He entered his first spring training with the White Sox, where he had to acclimate to new hitting coach Derek Shomon and play for Team Australia in the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>“It's been an eventful one, that's for sure,” Mead said Sunday. “I was really proud to go away and play for my country, but it made spring a little bit interrupted.”</p><p>Mead returned to Arizona this week after Australia went 2-2 in pool play and lost the tiebreaker. He was productive in the WBC, batting 5-for-14 with three RBIs in four games. He said the atmosphere at the Tokyo Dome was memorable.</p><p>“We had about two sections full,” he said of the fan support. “ It was interesting. [In the] Japan game, every time we would get out of a jam or have a couple of hits, the two sections above our dugout were going crazy, and the rest of the stadium was pretty quiet. And when they took the lead in the sixth or seventh, everyone was standing except for those two sections.</p><p>“The cool thing about those games is if the team wins, that’s all that really matters. It was super special to have a pretty successful tournament by our standards. It’s disappointing to come up a little bit short, but it was definitely a memorable experience.”</p><p>Playing for his country imbued Mead with a level of confidence in his performance and body, having already played five nine-inning games. Mead, who the Sox acquired at the trade deadline from the Rays in the Adrian Houser trade, is battling for a roster spot. He’s out of options, so he would have to be put on waivers to go to the minors.</p><p>Since joining the Sox, the organization has focused on getting Mead to unlock his power in-game. A former top-100 prospect, Mead is similar to Miguel Vargas when he was acquired by the club in 2024. Both showed glimpses of extra-base power but had yet to sustain it at the major-league level. Vargas turned the corner in May last season after a mechanical change that allowed him to be more on time at the plate — he hit 16 homers and 32 doubles last season.</p><p>Now, with the help of assistant hitting coach Joel McKeithan and hitting coach Derek Shomon, Mead is working on his launch angle to avoid hitting too many groundballs. He already changed his stance last season to be more upright at the plate to allow him to get his ‘A’ swing off more consistently.</p><p>“I lift the ball naturally, but it's about being efficient and getting to the contact point I want,” Mead said. “The environment [McKeithan Shomon] built in the cage has been really good. Sometimes it's challenging to get guys in there and work on their swing, but I think those two make it a good environment to work.”</p><p>Over six Cactus League games, Mead is batting .263/.263/.474 with one double and a homer.</p><p>“He’s been great,” manager Will Venable said. “ Watching Curtis last year, you watch him in BP, and you know the power that’s in there. He just didn’t really get to it in games last year. Coming into this year, [I was] hoping that you see some of the pop come out, and it has.”</p><p>After stepping up in games that are more stressful than games at Camelback Ranch, Mead isn’t too concerned with the looming roster decision.</p><p>”I just put my best foot forward and see what happens,” Mead said.<br></p>

entries.title: Illinois a No. 3 seed in NCAA Tournament, will face No. 14 Penn on Thursday in South Carolina
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Illinois’ men’s basketball</a> team will enter the NCAA Tournament as the No. 3 seed in the South Region and play No. 14 seed Penn on Thursday in Greenville, South Carolina.</p><p>The Illini, 24-8, have been a Top 25 team all season. They cracked the top 10 in November, got there again in late January and stayed there throughout February, topping out at No. 5. But they've struggled over their last nine games, losing five of them — four in overtime, including a Big Ten tournament quarterfinal loss to Wisconsin at the United Center.</p><p>Penn (18-11) — led by former longtime Iowa coach Fran McCaffery — was the surprise winner of the Ivy League tournament. The Illini-Quakers winner will meet North Carolina or VCU on Saturday.</p><p>Freshman guard Keaton Wagler has been one of college basetball's breakout stars and leads the team in scoring. He is surrounded by a roster with exceptional size — including 6-9 freshman David Mirkovic and twin seven-footers Tomislav and Zvonimir Ivisic — and leads an offense that ranks second in the nation in efficiency. Defense is much more of a concern for the Illini, whose struggles against athletic guards have been obvious at times. This is a team capable of both going on huge scoring blitzes and blowing big leads.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/13/illinois-illini-wisconsin-badgers-big-ten-ncaa-tournament-march-madness-big-dance" target="_blank" >With Big Dance next, is Illinois a fatally flawed team? Has to be asked after déjà vu loss to Wisconsin</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Coach Brad Underwood has maintained all season he has a Final Four team, but that's a level no Illini squad has reached since 2005. The Illini came close in 2024, making it to the Elite Eight, their best finish under Underwood, who's in his ninth season.</p><p>In the South, the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight rounds will be played in Houston. The Final Four is in Indianapolis.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: CPS investigating Taft High School employee’s ‘inappropriate’ interactions with students
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Public Schools’ inspector general’s office has launched an investigation into an employee at a Norwood Park neighborhood high school.</p><p>A staff member at William Howard Taft High School, 6530 W. Bryn Mawr Ave., has been accused of engaging “inappropriately with students,” according to a notice obtained by the Sun-Times. Taft Principal Ryan Glowacz sent the notice to students’ families Wednesday afternoon.</p><p>The employee has been removed from the school while the CPS inspector general’s office investigates, the notice said.</p><p>A final determination whether to reinstate the staff member will be made based on information learned during the investigation, Glowacz said.</p><p>“Please know that we are taking this situation seriously, and we remain committed to providing your children with a safe, positive learning environment where they can reach their full potential,” Glowacz said. “While I am unable to discuss details regarding the investigation, I am always available to discuss steps the district takes to keep students safe and matters related to your child.”</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather
